From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Jamaican reggae vocal group

Not to be confused with [Pioneer (band)](/wiki/Pioneer_(band) "Pioneer (band)").

| The Pioneers | |
| --- | --- |
| [Image of the Pioneers with (from left to right), Winston Hewitt, Sydney Crooks and Derrick Crooks](/wiki/File:The_Pioneers.jpeg "Image of the Pioneers with (from left to right), Winston Hewitt, Sydney Crooks and Derrick Crooks") Image of the Pioneers with (from left to right), Winston Hewitt, Sydney Crooks and Derrick Crooks | |
| Background information | |
| Origin | [Jamaica](/wiki/Jamaica "Jamaica") |
| Genres | [Reggae](/wiki/Reggae "Reggae"), [soul](/wiki/Soul_music "Soul music"), [rocksteady](/wiki/Rocksteady "Rocksteady") |
| Years active | 1962–present |
| Labels | [Trojan](/wiki/Trojan_Records "Trojan Records"), various others |
|  | |
| Members | [Sydney Crooks](/wiki/Sydney_Crooks "Sydney Crooks") George Agard aka George Dekker [Jackie Robinson](/wiki/Jackie_Robinson_(musician) "Jackie Robinson (musician)") |
|  | |
| Past members | Winston Hewitt Derrick Crooks Glen Adams |
|  | |
| Website | [Official website](http://www.pioneersreggaegroup.com/) |

**The Pioneers** are a [Jamaican](/wiki/Jamaica "Jamaica") [reggae](/wiki/Reggae "Reggae"), [soul](/wiki/Soul_music "Soul music") and [rocksteady](/wiki/Rocksteady "Rocksteady") vocal trio, whose main period of success was in the 1960s. The trio has had different line-ups, and still occasionally performs.[[1]](#cite_note-AMG-1)

## Career

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=The_Pioneers_(band)&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Career")]

### Founding and early years: 1962-67

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=The_Pioneers_(band)&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Founding and early years: 1962-67")]

The Pioneers were formed in 1962 by [brothers](/wiki/Sibling "Sibling") [Sydney](/wiki/Sydney_Crooks "Sydney Crooks") and Derrick Crooks, and their friend Winston Hewitt.[[1]](#cite_note-AMG-1) Their early recordings "Good Nanny" and "I'll Never Come Running Back to You" were self-produced at the Treasure Isle studio in [Kingston, Jamaica](/wiki/Kingston,_Jamaica "Kingston, Jamaica"), using money lent to the Crooks brothers by their mother and appeared on [Ken Lack](/wiki/Ken_Lack "Ken Lack")'s Caltone label.[[2]](#cite_note-KatzSF-2)[[3]](#cite_note-KatzPFB-3)

Several other singles followed, none of them hits, before Hewitt emigrated to [Canada](/wiki/Canada "Canada") in 1966. Hewitt was replaced for around a year by the former [Heptones](/wiki/The_Heptones "The Heptones") [Glen Adams](/wiki/Glen_Adams "Glen Adams").[[2]](#cite_note-KatzSF-2)

The Pioneers' early singles were not successful, and Sydney began promoting concerts, while Derrick took up a job with the Alcoa bauxite company.[[2]](#cite_note-KatzSF-2) The group broke up in mid-1967.

### Revival: 1967-68

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=The_Pioneers_(band)&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Revival: 1967-68")]

Sydney began working at [Joe Gibbs](/wiki/Joe_Gibbs_(record_producer) "Joe Gibbs (record producer)")' record shop, and through Gibbs, returned to recording. At his first session (to record "Give Me Little Loving"), with the other members of The Pioneers gone, Crooks recruited [Jackie Robinson](/wiki/Jackie_Robinson_(musician) "Jackie Robinson (musician)"), who he found outside the studio just before recording began.[[2]](#cite_note-KatzSF-2) Crooks later said of the encounter:

> "When I was about to voice the song I looked outside the studio and I saw a little boy sitting on a stone. I said 'Hey, come here man, you can sing?' He sang the harmony for 'Give Me Little Loving' and his name was Jackie Robinson. After that I said to him 'You are one of the Pioneers from today' and he became the lead singer of the Pioneers".[[3]](#cite_note-KatzPFB-3)

The new version of The Pioneers enjoyed success with singles such as "Longshot" (a track written and produced by [Lee "Scratch" Perry](/wiki/Lee_%22Scratch%22_Perry "Lee \"Scratch\" Perry") on Gibbs' behalf about a long-lived but unsuccessful racehorse),[[4]](#cite_note-Thompson-4) "Jackpot", "Catch the Beat", and "Pan Yu Machete" (an attack on Perry, who left Gibbs in 1968 to start working on his own productions).[[2]](#cite_note-KatzSF-2) Crooks and Robinson also recorded as The Soul Mates in 1967.[[4]](#cite_note-Thompson-4) The group parted ways with Gibbs after an argument and moved on to work with [Leslie Kong](/wiki/Leslie_Kong "Leslie Kong"), the first recording for Kong being "Samfie Man", a song about a confidence trickster, which topped the Jamaican singles chart.[[2]](#cite_note-KatzSF-2)

### The classic trio, and the move to the UK: 1969-77

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=The_Pioneers_(band)&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: The classic trio, and the move to the UK: 1969-77")]

After a few further singles with Kong, the group recruited [Desmond Dekker](/wiki/Desmond_Dekker "Desmond Dekker")'s half-brother George Agard to become a trio again.[[2]](#cite_note-KatzSF-2) Sydney Crooks and his former Pioneer brother Derrick, along with Winston Bailey also recorded as [The Slickers](/wiki/The_Slickers "The Slickers"),[[5]](#cite_note-Moskowitz-5) recording "Nana" for producer Neremiah Reid. The Pioneers scored again with a sequel to "Long Shot", "Long Shot (Kick De Bucket)".[[1]](#cite_note-AMG-1) When Kong heard that the horse had died (during its 203rd race),[[6]](#cite_note-Chang-6) he insisted that the group write a song about it; The song was written and recorded quickly and became an instant hit.[[2]](#cite_note-KatzSF-2)

The band was popular in the [United Kingdom](/wiki/United_Kingdom "United Kingdom"), particularly among [skinheads](/wiki/Skinhead "Skinhead"). "Long Shot Kick de Bucket" was a big hit in 1969, and led to a tour of the UK, during which they resolved to relocate there.[[2]](#cite_note-KatzSF-2) Their [cover](/wiki/Cover_version "Cover version") of [Jimmy Cliff](/wiki/Jimmy_Cliff "Jimmy Cliff")'s "[Let Your Yeah Be Yeah](/wiki/Let_Your_Yeah_Be_Yeah "Let Your Yeah Be Yeah")" made No. 5 in the [UK Singles Chart](/wiki/UK_Singles_Chart "UK Singles Chart") in 1971.[[1]](#cite_note-AMG-1)[[7]](#cite_note-British_Hit_Singles_&_Albums-7)

The band moved to the UK in 1970.[[8]](#cite_note-Larkin-8) Their third UK hit was "Give and Take", which reached No. 35 in January 1972.[[7]](#cite_note-British_Hit_Singles_&_Albums-7)

### Soul years: 1976-79

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=The_Pioneers_(band)&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Soul years: 1976-79")]

In 1976, the Pioneers teamed up with [Eddy Grant](/wiki/Eddy_Grant "Eddy Grant") for an album for [Mercury Records](/wiki/Mercury_Records "Mercury Records") called *Feel The Rhythm*, which featured a nude female model on its cover. Grant preferred to produce them as a soul group and they released a number of singles in that idiom, including "Broken Man", "Feel The Rhythm" and "My Good Friend James"

The change of style was a critical but not a commercial success and the band split up for a time in the late 1970s, with Crooks concentrating on production work and continuing with his brother in The Slickers, while Agard and Robinson continued to record, together on the album *George & Jackie Sing*, and separately.

### First reformation: 1979-89

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=The_Pioneers_(band)&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: First reformation: 1979-89")]

The group reformed in the late 1970s and continued until 1989, when they split again to concentrate on separate careers. "Long Shot Kick de Bucket" was [covered](/wiki/Cover_version "Cover version") by [The Specials](/wiki/The_Specials "The Specials") on their *[The Special AKA Live!](/wiki/The_Special_AKA_Live! "The Special AKA Live!")* [EP](/wiki/Extended_play "Extended play"), which was a UK [No. 1](/wiki/Chart-topper "Chart-topper") hit in 1980[[9]](#cite_note-British_Hit_Singles_&_Albums_2-9) and resulted in The Pioneers' original version being reissued (as a double A side with "[The Liquidator](/wiki/The_Liquidator_(instrumental) "The Liquidator (instrumental)")" by [Harry J Allstars](/wiki/Harry_J_Allstars "Harry J Allstars")) and reaching No. 42 in the UK Singles Chart.[[7]](#cite_note-British_Hit_Singles_&_Albums-7)

The Pioneers song "Starvation" was also covered on the "[Starvation/Tam Tam Pour L'Ethiopie](/wiki/Starvation/Tam_Tam_Pour_L%27Ethiopie "Starvation/Tam Tam Pour L'Ethiopie")" [charity](/wiki/Charitable_organization "Charitable organization") single released in 1985, which peaked at UK number 33. The Pioneers shared lead vocal duties on the single with members of [UB40](/wiki/UB40 "UB40"), with backing vocals by [General Public](/wiki/General_Public "General Public").

### Second reformation: 1999-present

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=The_Pioneers_(band)&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Second reformation: 1999-present")]

In 1999, the group reformed again and have continued to perform together since.

In 2005, the Pioneers performed at the [Maranhão](/wiki/Maranh%C3%A3o "Maranhão") Roots Reggae Festival in [São Luís](/wiki/S%C3%A3o_Lu%C3%ADs,_Maranh%C3%A3o "São Luís, Maranhão"), [Brazil](/wiki/Brazil "Brazil") before 15,000 fans.[[10]](#cite_note-Pioneers-10) The following year they appeared at the [Godiva Festival](/wiki/Godiva_Festival "Godiva Festival") in the [War Memorial Park](/wiki/War_Memorial_Park,_Coventry "War Memorial Park, Coventry"), [Coventry](/wiki/Coventry "Coventry"), [England](/wiki/England "England"). "Long Shot Kick de Bucket" was used in the 2008 [film](/wiki/Film "Film"), *[The Wackness](/wiki/The_Wackness "The Wackness")*.

In 2023, Jackie Robinson and George Dekker performed twice as the Pioneers at the London International Ska Festival.

## Discography

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=The_Pioneers_(band)&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Discography")]

### Albums

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=The_Pioneers_(band)&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Albums")]

* *Greetings from the Pioneers* - 1968 - Amalgamated Records - [produced](/wiki/Record_producer "Record producer") by [Joe Gibbs](/wiki/Joe_Gibbs_(record_producer) "Joe Gibbs (record producer)")
* *Long Shot* - 1969 - [Trojan Records](/wiki/Trojan_Records "Trojan Records") - produced by [Leslie Kong](/wiki/Leslie_Kong "Leslie Kong")
* *Battle of the Giants* - 1970 - Trojan Records - produced by Leslie Kong
* *Yeah!* - 1971 - Trojan Records
* *I Believe in Love* - 1972 - Trojan Records
* *Freedom Feeling* - 1973 - Trojan Records
* *I'm Gonna Knock on Your Door* - 1974 - Trojan Records
* *Feel the Rhythm* - 1976 - Mercury Records
* *Roll On Muddy River* - 1977 - Trojan Records
* *Pusher Man* - 1978 - Squad Disco
* *Pusher Man* - 1978 - Trojan Records (different tracks to the Squad Disco release)
* *Baby I Love You* - 1979 - Taretone
* *What a Feeling* - 1980 - Pioneer International
* *Reggae for Lovers* - 1982 - Vista Sounds
* *Reggae for Lovers Vol. 2* - 1983 - Vista Sounds
* *More Reggae for Lovers Vol. 3* - 1985 - Vista Sounds
* *More Reggae for Lovers Vol. 4* - 1985 - Vista Sounds

Compilations

* *From the Beginning 1969–1976* - Pioneer International
* *Kick de Bucket* - [Rhino Records](/wiki/Rhino_Records "Rhino Records")
* *Greatest Hits* - 1979 - Trojan Records
* *...Best of – Longshot Kick de Bucket* - 1997 - Trojan Records
* *Let Your Yeah Be Yeah: Anthology 1966 to 1986* - 2001 - Trojan Records
* *Give and Take: The Best of The Pioneers* - 2002 - Trojan Records

The Pioneers also had a number 42 UK hit in 1980 with a double-A-side release of "Long Shot Kick de Bucket" and [Harry J](/wiki/Harry_J "Harry J") All-Stars' "Liquidator",[[11]](#cite_note-auto-11) and a four-track EP consisting of tracks by The Pioneers, [The Maytals](/wiki/The_Maytals "The Maytals"), [The Skatalites](/wiki/The_Skatalites "The Skatalites"), and [Jimmy Cliff](/wiki/Jimmy_Cliff "Jimmy Cliff") reached number 86 in 1989.[[11]](#cite_note-auto-11)

## Cover versions

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=The_Pioneers_(band)&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Cover versions")]

The Pioneers track "Jackpot" was covered by [The Beat](/wiki/The_Beat_(British_band) "The Beat (British band)") on their 1980 [album](/wiki/Album "Album") *[I Just Can't Stop It](/wiki/I_Just_Can%27t_Stop_It_(album) "I Just Can't Stop It (album)")*. Their song "Starvation" was also covered on the "[Starvation/Tam Tam Pour L'Ethiopie](/wiki/Starvation/Tam_Tam_Pour_L%27Ethiopie "Starvation/Tam Tam Pour L'Ethiopie")" [charity](/wiki/Charitable_organization "Charitable organization") single released in 1985.

* [The Selecter](/wiki/The_Selecter "The Selecter") covered "Time Hard" as "Everyday" on their 1980 album *[Too Much Pressure](/wiki/Too_Much_Pressure_(album) "Too Much Pressure (album)").*
